concept_name: Relative Velocity Formula — V_AB = V_A − V_B
source_book: DC Pandey 6.10; NCERT 3.6 and 4.6 — split from relative_motion bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Two Rajdhani trains crossing on parallel tracks near Kanpur. You are sitting in train A moving at 120 km/h northward; train B comes past you at 100 km/h southward. Even though your speedometer says 120, the windows of train B whip past you at 220 km/h — that is the RELATIVE velocity you actually feel. Every passenger on the Indian Railway network has experienced V_AB in their bones.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A Mumbai local train moving at 40 km/h. A second local on the parallel track passes going the same direction at 50 km/h. From your seat the faster train creeps past at only 10 km/h — feels like it is barely moving, because its velocity RELATIVE to you is just the 10 km/h difference.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A NASA space shuttle docking with the ISS. Both are racing around Earth at 27,000 km/h, but their relative velocity near docking is just a few centimetres per second. Same physics, same formula — V_AB = V_A minus V_B — just with different frames.

V_A.name: Velocity of A in ground frame

V_B.name: Velocity of B in ground frame

V_AB.name: Velocity of A as seen from B

formulas.relative_definition: V_AB = V_A - V_B  (velocity of A as seen from an observer riding on B)
formulas.symmetry: V_AB = -V_BA  (swap the observer and object, the sign flips)
formulas.observer_rule: To compute the velocity seen by any observer, subtract the observer's velocity from the object's velocity.

constraints: Always subtract the OBSERVER's velocity, never add it.
Direction matters: sign is preserved through the subtraction.
If both observer and object have the same velocity, V_AB = 0 — the object appears stationary.

STATE_1.title: The Core Idea — Seeing A From B

scene_composition.text:
Velocity is ALWAYS 'relative to something'.

  When we say a car moves at 60 km/h,
  we silently mean 60 km/h relative to the ROAD.

  If we want to know how the car looks to
  another moving car (observer B),
  we need a new number: V_AB.

  Definition:
     V_AB  =  velocity of A as seen from B
           =  V_A  -  V_B

  Subtract the observer.
Two Rajdhani trains near Kanpur, opposite directions

tts_sentences.text_en: Every velocity you quote silently carries a reference frame — usually the ground.
When two observers disagree, we need a new number: the velocity of A as seen from B, written V AB.
The rule is beautifully compact. V AB equals V A minus V B — subtract the observer.
Picture two Rajdhani trains approaching near Kanpur. Train A moves north at 120; train B moves south at 100.
From your seat inside train A, the windows of train B fly past at 220 kilometres per hour — that is 120 minus minus 100, the relative velocity.

STATE_2.title: Worked Example — Two Mumbai Locals on Parallel Tracks

scene_composition.text:
Two Mumbai locals, SAME direction

  V_A  =  50 km/h  (faster train)
  V_B  =  40 km/h  (slower train)

  Sitting in train B, how fast does train A creep past?

     V_AB  =  V_A  -  V_B
            =  50  -  40
            =  10 km/h

  Train A appears to move at ONLY 10 km/h from B's window.
  Although ground sees it at 50.

  This is why overtaking on a highway feels 'slow'
  when both cars are going the same way.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a friendlier case — two locals running the same direction on parallel tracks.
Train A at 50 kilometres per hour, train B at 40, both northbound.
Relative velocity of A as seen from B is 50 minus 40, which is only 10 kilometres per hour.
This is why overtaking on the highway feels slow — the relative speed is only the DIFFERENCE, not the sum of the two speeds.

STATE_3.title: Symmetry — V_AB and V_BA are Opposite

scene_composition.text:
Swap observer and object:

  V_AB  =  V_A  -  V_B   =  +10 km/h
  V_BA  =  V_B  -  V_A   =  -10 km/h

  So  V_BA  =  -V_AB.

  Intuition:
    From train B's seat, train A creeps FORWARD at +10 km/h.
    From train A's seat, train B drifts BACKWARD at -10 km/h.
  The magnitudes match; the signs flip.

  This symmetry is free — never compute both directions
  from scratch, just flip the sign.

tts_sentences.text_en: One last free gift from the formula — the symmetry rule.
V AB equals V A minus V B. Swap the letters — V BA equals V B minus V A. That is minus V AB.
So if train A crawls forward at plus 10 as seen from B, train B drifts backward at minus 10 as seen from A. Magnitudes match, signs flip.
Never compute both directions from scratch — one subtraction, then flip the sign for the return view.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student tries to ADD the two velocities instead of subtracting — thinks relative = total.
confusion_patterns: relative speed is sum of speeds

scene_composition.text:
Student's instinct:

  Train A at 50 km/h, train B at 40 km/h.
  "Both are moving — let me ADD them:
   50 + 40 = 90 km/h relative velocity."

  But common sense immediately rejects this:
  If two trains moving the SAME way really had 90 km/h
  of separation speed, even slow overtakes would feel like
  rocket flybys. They don't. They feel slow.

  The instinct is wrong. Let us fix it.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the common error. A student reads the problem, sees two moving trains, and adds them.
50 plus 40 equals 90. Done — relative velocity is 90.
But your own lived experience rejects this. Same-direction overtakes feel SLOW, not like rocket flybys.
If relative motion really added like this, trains would explode past each other. They don't. Something is wrong with the instinct.

STATE_2.title: Why Subtraction — Think About the Observer's Frame

scene_composition.text:
Why subtraction, not addition:

  Step 1: Imagine you are the observer, riding on train B.
          From YOUR seat, train B is at REST — you do not see it move.
  Step 2: Your own velocity, as you experience it, is zero.
  Step 3: For that to be true mathematically, subtract V_B
          from EVERYTHING — including V_A.

    V_AB  =  V_A  -  V_B

  The subtraction is the mathematical act of 'entering train B's frame'.

  Same direction:    V_AB = 50 - 40 = 10 km/h      (slow overtake, matches reality)
  Opposite direction: V_AB = 50 - (-40) = 90 km/h  (fast approach, matches reality)
Subtraction = shifting into the observer's frame.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is why subtraction is the right move.
Imagine you are sitting on train B. From your own seat, train B is at rest — you do not feel it moving.
To make that true mathematically, you must subtract V B from every velocity in the universe — including V A.
That is what V AB equals V A minus V B means. You are mathematically shifting into train B's point of view.
Same direction: 50 minus 40 equals 10 — slow overtake. Opposite direction: 50 minus minus 40 equals 90 — fast approach. Both match real life.

scene_composition.text:
The sign convention handles it all:

  Step 1: Pick a positive direction (e.g. north = +).
  Step 2: Assign each velocity its own sign.
  Step 3: Plug into V_AB = V_A - V_B.
  Step 4: Interpret the SIGN of the answer.
          Positive -> A moves in + direction relative to B
          Negative -> A moves in - direction relative to B

  Same direction, both positive:  V_AB = 50 - 40 = +10 (small)
  Opposite direction, one negative: V_AB = 50 - (-40) = +90 (large)
  Same speed, same direction:     V_AB = 50 - 50 = 0   (stationary)

  No more addition. No more guessing.

tts_sentences.text_en: The sign convention fixes this mistake permanently.
Pick a positive direction. Assign every velocity its own sign. Subtract. Interpret the sign of the answer.
Same direction, both positive: 50 minus 40 equals 10 — small and slow.
Opposite direction: 50 minus minus 40 equals 90 — large and fast.
Same velocity: 50 minus 50 equals zero — the object appears stationary from your frame. One formula, three different answers, all correct.
